# 6.3 Visualizing Continuous Variables {.unnumbered}

For a single continuous variable, the main goal is usually to understand the distribution. You want to see where values are concentrated, whether the distribution is symmetric or skewed, and whether there are possible outliers.

Useful visualizations include:

- histogram
- density plot
- box plot
- violin plot
- jittered data points
- mean marker

## Histograms and Density Plots

A histogram shows how often values fall within ranges. It is useful for seeing the overall shape of a distribution.

A density plot smooths the distribution into a curve. In jamovi, you may be able to show a histogram with a density overlay. I like this because it gives a fuller picture of the variable.

Use histograms and density plots to ask:

- Is the distribution roughly symmetric?
- Is it positively or negatively skewed?
- Are values clustered in one part of the scale?
- Are there possible outliers?

## Box Plots, Violin Plots, Data Points, and Mean

jamovi also lets you combine several useful pieces of information:

- **Box plot**: shows the median, quartiles, and possible outliers
- **Violin plot**: shows the shape or density of the data
- **Data points**: show individual observations, often jittered so they do not overlap as much
- **Mean**: shows the average value

Personally, I often like selecting all of these together when exploring a continuous variable. This lets you see the data, the distribution, the middle 50%, and the mean at the same time. It gives a lot of information and helps avoid hiding weird things in the data.

## What to Look For

When looking at a continuous-variable graph, ask:

- Where are most values?
- Is the distribution roughly normal?
- Is there positive or negative skew?
- Are there outliers?
- Does the mean seem pulled away from the median?
- Do the values fall within the expected range?

::: {.callout-tip title="Check Your Understanding"}
A variable has most scores near the low end and a long tail toward higher scores. Is this positive skew or negative skew?

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>

This is positive skew because the long tail extends toward higher values.

</details>
:::
